perturbation.original_text: 3. FEES 3.1. Initial Franchise Fee. You must pay us an initial franchise fee of $30,000 when you sign this Agreement. The initial franchise fee is paid in consideration of the rights granted in Section 1 and is fully earned at the time paid. You acknowledge that we have no obligation to refund the initial franchise fee in whole or in part for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. FEES 3.1. Initial Franchise Fee. You must pay us an initial franchise fee when you sign this Agreement. The initial franchise fee is paid in consideration of the rights granted in Section 1 and is fully earned at the time paid. You acknowledge that we have no obligation to refund the initial franchise fee in whole or in part for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific amount of the initial franchise fee ($30,000), the text becomes ambiguous. While it still requires a payment, the contract no longer defines the amount, which create uncertainty in enforcement and contradicts other implicit sections, such as 14.3.4 [Transfer of Entire Business], where it refers to twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee, if greater
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.3.4. We must be paid, either by you or the transferee, a transfer fee ("Transfer Fee") in an amount equal to $7,500 (or twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee, if greater). The payment of this transfer fee is in place of any initial franchise fee due under the Franchise Agreement the transferee will enter under Section 14.3.2 [Transfer of Entire Business] above. If the transferee is a spouse, son, or daughter of the transferor and the transfer is for estate-planning purposes, no transfer fee is charged, but the transferor must reimburse us for the out-of-pocket expenses (including attorneys' fees) we incur in connection with reviewing, approving, and properly documenting the transfer.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the specific dollar amount ($30,000) of the initial franchise fee, which directly contradicts section 14.3.4. This section references 'twenty- five percent (25%) of the then-current initial franchise fee', creating a clear contradiction because the 'then-current initial franchise fee' is no longer a defined amount in the updated section 3.1. This omission introduces ambiguity and directly impacts the enforceability of the transfer fee calculation, leading to a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the Omissions - In Text Contradiction perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 9.2. Local Marketing. Beginning on the Opening Date, during each consecutive three-calendar-month period during the Term, you must spend three percent (3%) or more of your Gross Sales on local marketing of the Franchised Business. You must make these local marketing expenditures on a quarterly basis, based upon your Gross Sales calculated for the current year on an annualized basis. Your local spending obligation is in addition to your Marketing Fund contributions. Upon our request, you agree to submit to us, for our approval, an annual proposal and quarterly proposals detailing your plan for implementing your local marketing budget.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.2. Local Marketing. Beginning on the Opening Date, during each consecutive three-calendar-month period during the Term, you must spend on local marketing of the Franchised Business. You must make these local marketing expenditures on a quarterly basis, based upon your Gross Sales calculated for the current year on an annualized basis. Your local spending obligation is in addition to your Marketing Fund contributions. Upon our request, you agree to submit to us, for our approval, an annual proposal and quarterly proposals detailing your plan for implementing your local marketing budget.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific percentage (three percent (3%)) of Gross Sales that must be spent on local marketing, the obligation remains but is undefined. This creates an internal contradiction because while the section mandates local marketing spending, it gives no figure and relies on another external reference within the contract to dictate this percentage. Other sections reference this percentage (three percent (3%)) such as Section 9.2 [Local Marketing] itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.2. Local Marketing. Beginning on the Opening Date, during each consecutive three-calendar-month period during the Term, you must spend on local marketing of the Franchised Business. You must make these local marketing expenditures on a quarterly basis, based upon your Gross Sales calculated for the current year on an annualized basis. Your local spending obligation is in addition to your Marketing Fund contributions. Upon our request, you agree to submit to us, for our approval, an annual proposal and quarterly proposals detailing your plan for implementing your local marketing budget.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a mandatory spending amount (3%) of Gross Sales. The changed text removes the 3% requirement, creating an undefined obligation. The contradicted text, which is the same text as the changed text, now mandates local marketing spending without specifying an amount, contradicting the implied expectation that the original percentage still applies, leading to unenforceability. The definition of Omissions leading to in-text contradiction directly applies.

perturbation.original_text: 6.19.1. Types and Amounts of Coverage. Throughout the entire Term, you must maintain such types of insurance, in such amounts, as we may require. Such insurance is in addition to any other insurance that may be required by applicable law, your landlord, or otherwise. Policies that we require must be written by an insurance company reasonably satisfactory to us with an A.M. Best rating of "A" or better, and, must name us as an additional insured party. At a minimum, such policies must include the following: (a) commercial general liability insurance, completed-operations and independent-contractors coverage in the amount of $1,000,000, per person/per occurrence for bodily injury and property damage combined with a general aggregate of $3,000,000.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.19.1. Types and Amounts of Coverage. Throughout the entire Term, you must maintain such types of insurance, in such amounts, as we may require. Such insurance is in addition to any other insurance that may be required by applicable law, your landlord, or otherwise. Policies that we require must be written by an insurance company reasonably satisfactory to us with an A.M. Best rating of "A" or better, and, must name us as an additional insured party. At a minimum, such policies must include the following: (a) commercial general liability insurance, completed-operations and independent-contractors coverage per person/per occurrence for bodily injury and property damage combined with a general aggregate.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific coverage amounts ($1,000,000 per person/per occurrence, $3,000,000 general aggregate) for commercial general liability insurance, the clause becomes vague. While insurance is still required, the defined amounts are omitted, contradicting the detailed specifications implied by 'Types and Amounts of Coverage' and creates uncertainty about whether the remainder of the section is still enforcable as it creates an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.19.1. Types and Amounts of Coverage. Throughout the entire Term, you must maintain such types of insurance, in such amounts, as we may require. Such insurance is in addition to any other insurance that may be required by applicable law, your landlord, or otherwise. Policies that we require must be written by an insurance company reasonably satisfactory to us with an A.M. Best rating of "A" or better, and, must name us as an additional insured party. At a minimum, such policies must include the following: (a) commercial general liability insurance, completed-operations and independent-contractors coverage per person/per occurrence for bodily injury and property damage combined with a general aggregate.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific monetary amounts for the commercial general liability insurance coverage, contradicting the implicit expectation of defined amounts outlined in the 'Types and Amounts of Coverage' title. This omission creates a direct conflict and uncertainty, making the contradicted text's enforcement unclear, as the monetary limits are no longer specified, satisfying the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.
